title: Always-On VPN Enforcement
dataSources: VPN logs with `always_on` flag, MDM compliance
spl:
index=vpn sourcetype="cisco:asa" action="session_connect" earliest=-24h
| eval aov=if(match(lower(_raw),"(?i)always.?on|pre.?login"),1,0)
| lookup corp_laptops.csv hostname OUTPUT requires_aov
| where requires_aov=1 AND aov=0
| stats count by user, hostname, src
description: Identifies corporate assets connecting without Always-On (pre-login) VPN when policy requires it.
value: Compliance teams verify that endpoints required to maintain Always-On VPN connections are actually doing so, catching configuration drift or user tampering that creates unprotected internet access windows.
implementation: Prefer explicit field from ASA if available. Join MDM “managed device” list for `requires_aov`.
visualization: Table (non-compliant hosts), Bar chart (violations by OU), Single value (violation count).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA (`sourcetype=cisco:asa`). Data in `index=vpn`.
• Key fields: `user`, `action=session_connect`, always-on flag (parsed from raw or group policy).
• Cisco AnyConnect Always-On mode keeps the VPN connected at all times, preventing users from accessing the internet without going through the VPN. Critical for compliance in regulated industries.
• Create a `corp_laptops.csv` lookup: `user,device_type,always_on_required,compliance_group`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify always-on detection:
```spl
index=vpn sourcetype="cisco:asa" action="session_connect" earliest=-24h
| eval aov=if(match(lower(_raw), "always.?on|pre.?login"), 1, 0)
| stats count by aov, group_policy
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Always-On compliance:
```spl
index=vpn sourcetype="cisco:asa" action="session_connect" earliest=-24h
| eval aov=if(match(lower(_raw), "always.?on|pre.?login"), 1, 0)
| lookup corp_laptops.csv user OUTPUT always_on_required compliance_group
| where always_on_required="yes" AND aov=0
| stats count values(group_policy) as policies by user, compliance_group
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL: Identifies users who are required to use Always-On VPN but are connecting without it. This means they had internet access before the VPN connected — a compliance gap. Common cause: the AnyConnect profile on the endpoint does not have Always-On enabled, or the user disabled it (possible with admin rights on some configurations).

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Check an Always-On user's AnyConnect profile: Settings > Preferences > should show 'Always-On VPN' enabled.
(b) Compare with MDM data: devices enrolled in the Always-On profile should all show `aov=1`.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — Always-On compliance rate. Row 2 — Non-compliant users.
Alert: Daily compliance report.

Runbook:
1. **Non-compliant device** — Push the correct AnyConnect profile via MDM.
2. **User disabled Always-On** — Lock down the profile with `AllowDisableAlwaysOn=false`.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **Always-On indicator not in logs** — The ASA may not explicitly log Always-On status. Infer from the group policy name or the tunnel establishment timing (pre-login = Always-On).
• **AnyConnect profile not deploying** — Check SCCM/Intune deployment status.
grandmaExplanation: We make sure that laptops that are supposed to always stay connected to our secure system actually do, so employees never access the internet without our protections in place.
